Daniel's final vision closes not with explanation but with mystery—and with a promise meant to sustain God's people through whatever darkness lies ahead. The angel does not ease Daniel's confusion by clarifying every timeline or detail. Instead, he seals the knowledge itself, declaring that understanding will come only at "the time of the end." This refusal to provide complete comprehension is itself part of the message. God is telling Daniel, and through him all who read these words, that there are seasons when trust must exceed understanding. The deliverance promised in verse 1 is absolute—those whose names are written in the book will be saved—but the exact timing and manner remain hidden. This is not cruelty but mercy, because a people obsessed with decoding the schedule of the end would become paralyzed by speculation rather than strengthened by obedience.

The vision pivots sharply in verses 10 and 12. While the wicked will remain blind and continue in their wickedness, the wise will understand. This is not intellectual superiority; it is the understanding that comes from faith and from walking with God through present suffering. The blessing pronounced on those who "wait and reach the end of the 1,335 days" speaks to endurance through a specific, bounded trial. God is not speaking of eternity but of a concrete historical period of distress—severe, unprecedented, but finite. The number itself matters less than what it signifies: that affliction has an end, that persistence through that period is possible, and that those who endure will not find their faithfulness mocked. The wise are blessed not because they know what will happen, but because they trust who will make it happen.

Daniel himself receives his final word in verse 13: rest. After visions, after standing in the presence of angelic beings, after receiving knowledge of centuries yet to unfold, Daniel is told simply to go his way until the end comes for him. Then he will rest, and then he will arise to inherit his portion when all things are renewed. The reflection offered here is starkly different from what the modern reader might expect. There is no triumph narrative in which Daniel sees the fulfillment of what he has witnessed. Instead, there is the invitation to die in faith, to trust that the God who showed him the future will complete what He has promised. This is the deepest challenge of Daniel 12: not to decode the future, but to surrender to a God whose plans exceed our understanding and whose faithfulness is not diminished by our confusion. Daniel's inheritance awaits not in this age but in the age to come. And so does yours, if you have fixed your hope in Jesus Christ, in whom all that the prophets foresaw finds its fulfillment.
